selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":49,"gender":"man","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"carpenter","city_of_living":"Kansas City","employment_status":"employed full-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"bachelor's degree","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":1,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"normal","religious_beliefs":"Agnostic","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["birdwatching","baking","travel","yoga","gardening"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":28,"level":"low"},"conscientiousness":{"score":34,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":2,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":61,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":1,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"When you're used to the solid, tangible things—like the grain of good oak or the right way to lay a foundation—the whole business of social media feels like trying to build something with smoke. It's just not real. Dealing with all that fake content, the stuff that’s just spun up to stir the pot, it really comes down to slowing down and using your common sense, the same way you’d inspect a load of lumber before you start framing. You can’t rush the truth, and you can’t build a good structure on a bad piece of wood. My take is that the best defense isn't some fancy algorithm; it’s a disciplined skepticism. Before you let something sink in, whether it’s a headline or a picture, you need to treat it like an unverified blueprint. Does the source have a track record? Are they reporting facts or just throwing out opinions dressed up as news? I tend to look for consistency, just like I check if all my measurements line up before I make a cut. If it sounds too sensational, if it’s making you instantly furious or overly giddy, that’s usually a sign that the person trying to push it doesn't care about accuracy; they care about the reaction. It takes a bit of mental effort, like taking time to properly prune a rose bush rather than just hacking at it, but that deliberate pause is what separates the solid fact from the flimsy fabrication. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: When you're used to the solid, tangible things—like the grain of good oak or the right way to lay a foundation—the whole business of social media feels like trying to build something with smoke. It's just not real. Dealing with all that fake content, the stuff that’s just spun up to stir the pot, it really comes down to slowing down and using your common sense, the same way you’d inspect a load of lumber before you start framing. You can’t rush the truth, and you can’t build a good structure on a bad piece of wood. My take is that the best defense isn't some fancy algorithm; it’s a disciplined skepticism. Before you let something sink in, whether it’s a headline or a picture, you need to treat it like an unverified blueprint. Does the source have a track record? Are they reporting facts or just throwing out opinions dressed up as news? I tend to look for consistency, just like I check if all my measurements line up before I make a cut. If it sounds too sensational, if it’s making you instantly furious or overly giddy, that’s usually a sign that the person trying to push it doesn't care about accuracy; they care about the reaction. It takes a bit of mental effort, like taking time to properly prune a rose bush rather than just hacking at it, but that deliberate pause is what separates the solid fact from the flimsy fabrication. You have to cultivate that steady, moderate approach, the kind of focus you get when you’re out in the yard watching the birds settle in after a rain; you observe, you assess, and you don't jump to conclusions based on a quick glance.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a practical, low-extraversion, conscientious carpenter who is skeptical of fast-paced, abstract concepts like social media (given the stated 'no social media' usage). The opinion must reflect this grounded, methodical, and cautious nature. The analogy used must relate to carpentry, building, or gardening (hobbies). The tone should be measured, pragmatic, and slightly world-weary regarding digital chaos.
